coredata.dc:title: Hybrid nanosensors of carbon quantum dots and fluorescent nanodiamonds: Ratiometric thermometry and multicolor sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Shiraya, Kota
Nakane, Yurina
Abe, Hiroshi
Ohshima, Takeshi
Sugi, Takuma
Yoshida, Yumi
Maeda, Kohji
Sotoma, Shingo
coredata.dc:description: Hybridization of carbon-based materials opens new frontiers in creating multifunctional nanosensors for biomedical and quantum sensing applications. Here, we present a novel nanosensor, CQD-FND, integrating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and fluorescent nanodiamonds (FNDs). By utilizing a silica coating to facilitate the conjugation of FNDs and CQDs, we developed a design that effectively suppresses fluorescence quenching of CQDs caused by dangling bonds on the FND surface, enabling sufficient fluorescence measurement from both FNDs and CQDs in the hybrids. This sensor achieves ratiometric thermometry, overcoming the limitations associated with using FNDs or CQDs individually for temperature measurements. The CQD-FND sensor exhibits remarkable stability under varying environmental conditions such as ionic strength, viscosity, and pH, demonstrating robust performance in biological systems and enabling accurate temperature measurements. Moreover, we labeled FNDs with blue- or green-fluorescent CQDs, enabling multicolor labeling for magnetic resonance measurements. We successfully measured the magnetic resonance signal of CQD-FND in cells and nematode C. elegans, demonstrating its potential for multi-parameter quantum biosensing applications. These results establish CQD-FND as a versatile tool for applications ranging from bioimaging to biosensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-vacancy canter
Silica coating
Ratio sensor
Magnetic resonance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell culture and their CQD-FND uptake
C. elegans culture and their CQD-FND uptake
Synthesis and fluorescence properties of b-CQD
Preparation of bright FND and FND-SiO2
Preparation and characterization of b-CQD-FND hybrid
All-optical ratiometric temperature measurement using b-CQD-FND
Multicolor labeling of FNDs and their ODMR measurement

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge Dr. Yoshiyuki Nonoguchi for his kind support with the FTIR and Raman spectroscopy measurements. This work was supported by Early-Career Scientists ( 19K16089 and 21K15053 to S.S.), Iketani Science and Technology Foundation ( 0351017 -A to S.S.), JSPS KAKENHI Grant-in-Aid for Transformative Research Areas B ( JP23H03845 to T.S.), for Scientific Research (A) ( 24H00577 to T.S.), JST-FOREST Program ( JPMJFR214R to T.S.).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photographs of b-CQD under daylight and UV light. (b) Two-dimensional fluorescence excitation-emission map of b-CQD. (c) Temperature-dependent fluorescence spectra (Ex: 320 nm) of b-CQD from 15 to 50 °C. The dotted line indicates wavelength at 425 nm. (d) Emission intensity of b-CQDs at 425 nm for each temperature. (e) Fluorescence emission intensity (Ex: 320 nm, Em: 425 nm) of b-CQDs during temperature cycles between 25 and 55 °C.

(a) Energy diagram of NVC. (b) Fluorescence spectra of FNDs with different electron irradiation doses. The dotted line indicates wavelength at 640 nm. (c) Emission intensity of FNDs at 640 nm for each electron irradiation dose. (d) ODMR spectrum of a single FND irradiated at 1.5 × 1019 cm−2 on a coverslip.
(a) FT-IR and (b) XPS spectra of FND and FND-SiO2. (c) TEM image of FND-SiO2.

(a) Fluorescence images of b-CQD, FND, and b-CQD-FND excited with 352 nm and 559 nm. (b) Fluorescence spectra of b-CQD (left) and b-CQD-FND (right) excited at 320 nm. (c) Size distribution of FND (left) and b-CQD-FND (right) measured by DLS at 0, 2, and 4 min after dispersion.

(a) Fluorescence intensity of b-CQD-FND at different concentrations: Em. 425 nm at Ex. 320 nm for b-CQD fluorescence and Em. 640 nm at Ex. 532 nm for FND fluorescence. (b) Fluorescence intensity of b-CQD and fluorescence intensity ratio of b-CQD-FND based on 400 μg mL−1. (c) Fluorescence spectra and fluorescence intensity ratio of b-CQD-FND at different temperatures. (d) Fluorescence intensity ratio of b-CQD-FND at 25 and 55 °C.

Relative changes in fluorescence ratio of b-CQD-FND under various conditions compared to those in water. (a) NaCl and KCl solution, (b) changes in viscosity due to glycerol, (c) pH change, and (d) changes in protein concentration due to BSA. On the right in (d) is the temperature dependence of the fluorescence ratio of b-CQD-FND under albumin concentration at 1000 μg mL−1.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of b-CQD and g-CQD, highlighting the distinct emission wavelengths of blue and green CQDs. The shaded background represents the wavelength range used in the simulations shown in (c–f). (b) The illustrations of b-CQD-FND and g-CQD-FND. (c-f) Simulations demonstrating the discrimination of FNDs using fluorescently labeled CQDs. (c) An illustration of b-CQD-FND and g-CQD-FND on the microscopic observation field. (d) The fluorescence intensity of each CQD-FND excited by 532 nm, (e) the fluorescence intensity distribution in the 370–430 nm range under 320 nm excitation, and (f) the fluorescence intensity distribution in the 500–650 nm range under 365 nm excitation. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) ODMR spectra of b-CQD-FND and g-CQD-FND on cover slips (upper panel) and the temperature dependence of the resonant frequency (lower panel). Bright field and fluorescence images and ODMR spectra of CQD-FNDs samples in (b) cells and (c) C. elegans. The bright field and fluorescence images in (b) and (c) are typical example using b-CQD-FND. The red circles in the microscope image are fluorescence from b-CQD-FND, and ODMR spectra were obtained from these particles. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Hybrid nanosensors of carbon quantum dots and fluorescent nanodiamonds: Ratiometric thermometry and multicolor sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate School of Science and Technology, Kyoto Institute of Technology, Hashikami-cho, Matsugasaki, Sakyo, Kyoto, 606-8585, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graduate School of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Program of Biomedical Science, Graduate School of Integrated Sciences for Life, Hiroshima University, Kagamiyama, Higashihiroshima, Hiroshima, 739-0046, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Institutes for Quantum Science and Technology (QST), Quantum Materials and Application Research Center (QUARC), 1233 Watanuki, Takasaki, Gunma, 370-1292, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Institutes for Quantum Science and Technology (QST)
Quantum Materials and Application Research Center (QUARC)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Institutes for Quantum Science and Technology (QST), Quantum Materials and Application Research Center (QUARC), 1233 Watanuki, Takasaki, Gunma 370-1292, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science, Tohoku University, Sendai, Miyagi, 980-8579, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science, Tohoku University, Sendai, Miyagi 980-8579, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hybridization of carbon-based materials opens new frontiers in creating multifunctional nanosensors for biomedical and quantum sensing applications. Here, we present a novel nanosensor, CQD-FND, integrating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and fluorescent nanodiamonds (FNDs). By utilizing a silica coating to facilitate the conjugation of FNDs and CQDs, we developed a design that effectively suppresses fluorescence quenching of CQDs caused by dangling bonds on the FND surface, enabling sufficient fluorescence measurement from both FNDs and CQDs in the hybrids. This sensor achieves ratiometric thermometry, overcoming the limitations associated with using FNDs or CQDs individually for temperature measurements. The CQD-FND sensor exhibits remarkable stability under varying environmental conditions such as ionic strength, viscosity, and pH, demonstrating robust performance in biological systems and enabling accurate temperature measurements. Moreover, we labeled FNDs with blue- or green-fluorescent CQDs, enabling multicolor labeling for magnetic resonance measurements. We successfully measured the magnetic resonance signal of CQD-FND in cells and nematode C. elegans, demonstrating its potential for multi-parameter quantum biosensing applications. These results establish CQD-FND as a versatile tool for applications ranging from bioimaging to biosensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanodiamonds, fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphite, and carbon quantum dots are all carbon-based allotropes. They exhibit markedly different properties in terms of physical, chemical, electrical, and optical characteristics, making these materials indispensable in various fields such as electronics, photonics, energy, purification, sensing, and biomedical applications [1]. Recently, attention has shifted towards the hybridization of these carbon materials, as hybrid systems offer new opportunities for enhancing performance and enabling applications that are difficult or impossible to achieve with individual materials alone [2]. For example, graphene-diamond hybrid films process graphene's high electrical conductivity with diamond's superior hardness, offering potential applications in advanced electronics, thermal management, and durable coatings [3]. Hybrid films of carbon nanotubes and graphite demonstrate exceptional mechanical strength and electrical conductivity, making them ideal for flexible electronic devices [4]. Despite the great advantages of hybrid carbon materials, hybridization of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and fluorescent nanodiamonds (FNDs) has not yet been reported, and their potential remains unexplored.

CQDs are a new class of fluorescent nanomaterial with a size less than 10 nm and considered a promising alternative to traditional metal-based quantum dots [5]. They are primarily consist of carbon and can be synthesized through hydrothermal synthesis, microwave (MW) irradiation, or pyrolysis [6]. CQDs emit fluorescence and their emission wavelength varies depending on particle size and surface chemical states [7]. They exhibit unique properties such as photostability and biocompatibility, which make them attractive for applications in bioimaging and biosensing [8–11]. It is also used in biosensors and electrocatalysts, and in a wide range of other applied technologies [12–15]. FNDs are diamond-nanoparticle with a size typically 50–200 nm and contain nitrogen-vacancy centers (NVCs) as lattice defects composed of nitrogen atoms and adjacent vacancies [16,17]. NVC has two different charge states: neutral NVC (NV0) and negatively-charged NVC (NV−). Both NVC emit fluorescence but only NV− has unique magnetic property [17]. FND emit stable fluorescence in the 600–800 nm range showing no photobleaching and photoblinking, making them ideal probe for long-term imaging applications [16]. Furthermore, the quantum state of NV− can be detected via optically detected magnetic resonance (ODMR). In ODMR measurements, FNDs can function as highly sensitive nanoscale sensors for physical parameters such as temperature, pH, radical, and viscosity inside single cells [18–20].

Herein, we report the synthesis of unprecedented CQD-FND hybrids. We found that CQDs and FNDs do not bind effectively through physical adsorption. Furthermore, although we attempted direct conjugation via carbodiimide chemistry, this approach resulted in severe fluorescence quenching of the CQDs, making it impractical for this system. By employing a silica-coating-based strategy, we successfully conjugated CQDs and FNDs without unfavorable effect on their fluorescence properties. Using the CQD-FND, we demonstrate two unique potentials for biosensing applications.

First, we demonstrate its performance as an optical temperature sensor that overcomes the limitations in using CQDs and FNDs independently for temperature sensing. Since CQD fluorescence is temperature-dependent, they serve as fluorescence intensity-based thermometers [6]. However, one of the challenges is that fluorescence intensity is also influenced by their own concentration, making it difficult to distinguish between changes in temperature and concentration. FNDs can also be employed for temperature sensing through ODMR measurements, but this method requires the MW irradiation to induce magnetic resonance, which complicates experimental setup [21–23]. In this study, we demonstrate that CQD-FND hybrid functions as a MW-free ratiometric temperature sensor. In the CQD-FND hybrid system, the temperature-sensitive CQD fluorescence intensity is calibrated against the less temperature-sensitive FND fluorescence intensity, allowing for accurate temperature measurements that are independent of their concentration changes.

Second, we demonstrate color labeling of FNDs for multiparameter ODMR sensing, by altering the fluorescence color of CQDs in the CQD-FND hybrid structure. Previous studies have shown that FND-based ODMR sensing enables the monitoring of the physical parameters in biological systems [18]. For example, temperature change during neural differentiation, thermal conductivity in a single cell, cell membrane viscosity, and radical generation from mitochondria have been reported so far. However, these studies focus on measuring a single physical parameter within cells. Future research will need to address how multiple physicochemical parameters interact and influence each other, such as examining how mitochondrial heat production affects the viscosity of liquid droplets. That said, it is challenging to distinguish particles solely based on the red emission from FNDs. However, by labeling FNDs with CQDs of different emission colors, long-term multi-parameter ODMR measurements become feasible. In this study, we prepared FNDs labeled with blue-emitting CQDs and FNDs labeled with green-emitting CQDs and confirmed that these labeling has no adverse effect on the ODMR signal of the FNDs. Additionally, we successfully measured the ODMR signal in vitro, in cells, and in C. elegans.

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APS) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Citric acid, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), ethanol, and p-phenylenediamine (p-PD) were purchased from Nacalai Tesque. Ammonia solution was acquired from Wako Pure Chemical, and nanodiamonds (Micron + MDA, 0–100 nm) were purchased from Element Six.

To synthesize CQD that emit blue light (b-CQD), 100 μL of APS and citric acid (11, 22, 55, 110, 220 mg) were dissolved in 10 mL of purified water and sonicated for 1 min, and subjected to hydrothermal synthesis (12 h, 130, 150, 180 °C) using an electric furnace (Nitto Kagaku). After the reaction, the solution was filtered with a 220 nm syringe filter and subsequently lyophilized.

CQD that emit green light (g-CQD) that is used as a fluorescent label in ODMR measurements. g-CQD was synthesized using 30 mg of p-PD, 100 μL of APS, and 55 mg of citric acid as precursors through hydrothermal synthesis at 180 °C for 12 h, following the same procedure as for b-CQD. p-PD is one of the precursors used to shift the emission wavelength of CQDs to the longer wavelength side [24].

Nanodiamonds were irradiated with an electron beam (2 MeV) at doses of 0.2, 0.4, 1.0, and 1.5 × 1019 cm−2 to create vacancies in diamond crystal. The irradiated samples were annealed at 800 °C in a vacuum for 2 h, followed by air oxidation at 550 °C for 2 h, yielding FNDs.

Twelve milligrams of FND and 100 mg of PVP were dissolved in 200 mL of purified water and sonicated for 3 min. The mixture was stirred for 24 h, followed by centrifugation at 14,200 g for 10 min (Kubota). After discarding the supernatant, the precipitate was resuspended in 40 mL of purified water. This washing was repeated three times. The final pellet was resuspended in 40 mL of ethanol, centrifuged at 14,200 g for 10 min, and the supernatant was discarded. The pellet was dissolved in 12 mL of ethanol, followed by the addition of 120 μL of TEOS and 500 μL of ammonia solution. The mixture was stirred for 20 h and washed by centrifugation with ethanol three times, yielding FND-SiO2. To the precipitated FND-SiO2, 4 mL of b-CQD or g-CQD solution (10 mg mL−1) and 1 mL of ammonia water were added and dissolved in 12 mL of ethanol. After stirring the mixture for 20 h and subsequent washing process, CQD-FND samples were obtained.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the samples were measured using an FT/IR-4700typeA (Jasco) with a resolution of 4 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out using a JPS-9010series (Jeol), with MgKα radiation on samples mounted on indium foil. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured with a HD-2700 (Hitachi) with samples on copper grids. Confocal laser microscopy was performed using a FLUOVIEW FV10i (Olympus), using 352 nm light to excite the CQDs and 559 nm light for the FNDs. Raman spectroscopy measurements were measured using an NRS-4500 (Jasco), using 785 nm laser. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was measured using an ELSZ-1000ZS (Otsuka Electronics), with 50 integrations repeated every 2 min for a total of three measurements, evaluating particle size distribution over time.

Fluorescence spectra were measured using a spectrophotometer FP-8550 (Jasco), and sensitivity is optimized for each sample. The two-dimensional fluorescence spectra of the b-CQD were measured at excitation wavelengths of 300–450 nm and emission wavelengths of 400–550 nm. The temperature dependence of b-CQD was measured by increasing the temperature by 5 °C from 15 to 50 °C. Photostability was assessed by repeating the temperature change between 25 and 55 °C 10 times. Measurements were taken at 15–20 min per cycle. Fluorescence spectral measurements of FND were performed at an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. Ratio-type measurements of b-CQD-FND were evaluated at two excitation wavelengths of 320 and 532 nm. The temperature dependence was measured by increasing the temperature by 5 °C from 15 to 55 °C. The measurements of environmental factors such as ionic strength, viscosity, protein, and pH, were evaluated in comparison with that in water. NaCl and KCl were used for measurements in salt solution (10–1000 mM). Viscosity was adjusted by glycerol concentration (0.1–10 wt%). pH (1–13) was adjusted using HCl and NaOH. Protein concentrations (10–1000 μg mL−1) were adjusted by adding BSA.

Fluorescence simulation of CQD-FND was performed using the fluorescence spectrum of each CQD. Excitation light of 320 nm was used for the b-CQD measurement and 365 nm for the g-CQD measurement. Simulations were performed by integrating the fluorescence intensities at 370–430 nm and 500–650 nm, respectively. In the simulation results, the height of the bumps in the figure corresponds to the integrated fluorescence intensity.

ODMR measurements were performed using FNDs and a homebuilt microscope. A continuous-wave neodymium-doped yttrium aluminum garnet (Nd:YAG) laser at 532 nm (Laser Quantum, gem 532) was used to illuminate the FNDs, initializing and reading out the spin state of NVCs. Fluorescence emitted from the NVCs was collected using an oil immersion objective lens (Evident, UPLXAPO, 60x, NA1.42), passed through a long-pass filter to remove the excitation light, and imaged with a qCMOS camera (Hamamatsu Photonics, ORCA-Quest).

A copper coil, approximately 1 mm in diameter, was positioned just above the coverslip to deliver MW irradiation at frequencies near the electron spin resonance of the NVCs. The MW pulses were generated by a MW generator (Keysight, N5181A MXG) and amplified using linear MW power amplifiers (Mini Circuits, ZHL-16W-43-S+). Synchronization between the MW irradiation and image acquisition was achieved using a function generator (NF Corporation, WF1974). ODMR spectra were recorded across a range of MW frequencies by calculating the ratio in fluorescence intensities with and without MW irradiation. The sample temperature was controlled using a lens heater (Tokai Hit, TPiE-LH).

Fluorescence images were recorded while the MW frequency was digitally swept from 2820 to 2920 MHz in increments of 1 MHz. The qCMOS camera exposure time was adjusted to 40 ms in HCImage software (Hamamatsu Photonics). For each MW frequency, a set of images was accumulated 30 times for glass slide, HeLa cell and C. elegans experiments. This process yielded ODMR spectra from isolated CQD-FND particles within the field of view. Each spectrum was fitted using a six-parameter superposition of two Lorentzian functions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and their CQD-FND uptake
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells were cultured in 50 mm glass-based dished with Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium supplemented with 10 % fetal calf serum and 1 % antibiotic-antimycotic at 37 °C with 5 % CO2. The CQD-FND samples were dispersed in culture medium at a concentration of 10 μg mL−1 and then incubated overnight. After extensive wash by PBS, the cells were observed under the ODMR microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: C. elegans culture and their CQD-FND uptake
ce:section.ce:para: C. elegans were maintained and handled by using standard methods [25]. The lite-1(ce314) strain was used as a light insensitive mutant [26]. Worms were grown to the L4 stage at 20 °C on a nematode growth medium (NGM) plate seeded with Escherichia coli (E. coli) OP50 as a food source. To feed the CQD-FND to the worms, 100 μL of 10 μg mL−1 CQD-FND suspension in sterile water was seeded on the bacteria lawn (3 cm × 4 cm rectangle) in an NGM plate, and twenty L4 stage worms were cultivated overnight before the ODMR measurements. For measurements, worms were placed to a 6.7 % agar pad formed on a coverslip, immersed in sodium azide, and sandwiched with coverslips.

Scheme 1 depicts the schematic illustration for the synthesis of b-CQD-FND. The synthesis of b-CQDs, electron irradiation for creating NVCs and silica coating on FNDs, and hybridization process of b-CQDs and FND are described. We herein present the potential of b-CQD-FND as an all-optical thermometer and multicolor labeling of FNDs for ODMR sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and fluorescence properties of b-CQD
ce:section.ce:para: b-CQDs were synthesized from APS and citric acid via hydrothermal synthesis (Scheme 1: b-CQD). The optimal conditions for hydrothermal synthesis, including temperature and the APS-to-citric acid ratio, were investigated to achieve the highest fluorescence intensity (Fig. S1). The optimal conditions were determined to be 180 °C for 12 h with an APS-to-citric acid ratio of 1 : 0.5, which were subsequently used in further experiments. The obtained CQDs appeared transparent under daylight and emitted blue fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 1 a). Fig. 1b shows the two-dimensional fluorescence excitation-emission map of the b-CQDs, with maximum fluorescence intensity at 425 nm when excited by 320 nm.

Next, we evaluated the performance of the b-CQDs as temperature sensors. Fig. 1c presents the changes in the fluorescence spectra of the b-CQDs as the temperature was increased in 5 °C step from 15 to 50 °C. As shown in Fig. 1d, the fluorescence intensity decreased linearly with increasing temperature, demonstrating that b-CQDs can function as fluorescence intensity-based temperature sensors. Notably, the fluorescence intensity remained stable even after 10 cycles of temperature changes between 25 and 50 °C, without reduction in the temperature sensitivity (Fig. 1e). These results indicate that b-CQDs allows for long-term fluorescence imaging and temperature sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of bright FND and FND-SiO2
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 2 a illustrates the energy diagram of the NVC. The ground state of the NVC is spin triplet and split into two quantum states, m
s = 0 and m
s = ±1. Electrons excited from either state emit red fluorescence as they relax, however, some electrons excited from the m
s = ±1 state undergo intersystem crossing to a singlet state, allowing them to relax without fluorescence emission. This unique property enables ODMR, where magnetic resonance transitions between m
s = 0 and m
s = ±1 are detected as changes in fluorescence intensity.

The fluorescence intensity and ODMR properties of FND depend on the number of NVCs, and electron irradiation is one of the methods for increasing the number of NVCs in diamond (Scheme 1: FND). We investigated how fluorescence spectra of 100 nm-sized FNDs changes with varying doses of electron irradiation, specifically at 0.2 × 1019, 0.4 × 1019, 1.0 × 1019, and 1.5 × 1019 cm−2 (Fig. 2b). Fig. 2c displays the dose-dependent changes in the fluorescence intensity of the zero-phonon line (ZPL) of NV−, which contribute to ODMR sensing and is observed around 640 nm (dotted line in Fig. 2b). The ZPL fluorescence intensity increased with irradiation, showing the highest fluorescence at a dose of 1.5 × 1019 cm−2, though it nearly reached a plateau at a dose of 1.0 × 1019 cm−2.

Next, to verify whether FNDs irradiated with a dose of 1.5 × 1019 cm−2 could be used for sensing application, we measured the ODMR spectrum. Excessive electron irradiation is known to degrade crystal quality and diminish the quality of the ODMR signal. However, under these conditions, we were able to obtain a clear ODMR signal: A decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed around 2870 MHz (Fig. 2d), corresponding to magnetic resonance between m
s = 0 and m
s = ±1. Based on these results, FNDs irradiated with a dose of 1.5 × 1019 cm−2 were used in subsequent experiments.

We next coated FND surface with SiO2 (Scheme 1: FND-SiO2), and confirmed the synthesis using FT-IR, XPS, and TEM. In the FT-IR spectrum of FND-SiO2, peaks attributed to Si-O and Si-O-Si bonds were observed around 1000–1300 cm−1 (Fig. 3 a). Similarly, after coating, a decrease in the C1s orbital signal and an increase in the signal intensities of the O1s, Si2s and Si2p orbitals were observed in the XPS measurement. (Fig. 3b). The TEM image showed a clear silica coating with a measured thickness of approximately 10–20 nm (Fig. 3c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of b-CQD-FND hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: The b-CQDs contain Si-O and Si-O-Si bonding derived from the precursors, while the FNDs are coated with silica. We here developed a novel synthesis strategy to conjugate FNDs and b-CQDs through silane coupling. The b-CQD-FND was obtained by reacting b-CQD and FND-SiO2 with ammonia in ethanol (Scheme 1). The conjugation was confirmed using a confocal microscope (Fig. 4 a). Under 352 nm excitation, fluorescence from b-CQDs dispersed on glass slide was observed, while FNDs were not excited and showed no detectable fluorescence. In contrast, under 559 nm excitation, fluorescence was observed from FND, with no emission detected from b-CQDs. When the b-CQD-FND hybrid was excited with each of these wavelengths, fluorescence was observed from both b-CQDs and FNDs and the emissions were colocalized, confirming the successful synthesis of b-CQD-FND. Conjugation of b-CQD and FND was also confirmed by XPS and Raman spectroscopy measurements (Fig. S2). Additionally, although the fluorescence spectrum becomes slightly broadened, the peak position of the b-CQD fluorescence remains almost unchanged before and after conjugation (Fig. 4b). Based on the fluorescence intensity, we estimated the number of b-CQDs on a single FND particle to be approximately 6500. For this calculation, we assumed that the CQDs and FNDs have sizes of 3 nm and 100 nm, respectively, and that both are perfectly spherical.

Next, we evaluated the dispersibility of the b-CQD-FND using DLS (Fig. 4c). FNDs without any surface modification formed aggregates in water, with particle sizes increasing from 100 nm to approximately 100,000 nm within 2 min. In contrast, b-CQD-FND didn't form aggregates, and particle size remained stable. The enhanced dispersibility is attributed to the hydrophilic SiO2 coating on the FND surface, which stabilizes the hybrid in aqueous environments. This high dispersibility is an indispensable property for the practical biological applications.

Notably, physical adsorption alone did not result in the formation of stable CQD-FND hybrids, as no detectable conjugation was observed under our experimental conditions. We further attempted the conjugation through amide coupling between the COOH groups on the FNDs and the amine groups on the b-CQDs using carbodiimide chemistry (Fig. S3). However, sufficient fluorescence from b-CQD on FND was not obtained. This is likely due to the quenching of the b-CQDs by the graphite or other defect on the FND surface and/or aggregation of the hybrids. We believe that employment of a silica coating was essential for achieving bright fluorescence from the b-CQDs in the hybrids, as it hinders aggregation and makes a suitable distance between the FND and b-CQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: All-optical ratiometric temperature measurement using b-CQD-FND
ce:section.ce:para: The potential of b-CQD-FND as a ratiometric temperature sensor was evaluated. The fluorescence intensity ratio of b-CQD to FND in the b-CQD-FND hybrid is expected to be independent of their concentration. Fig. 5 a shows the fluorescence intensity of b-CQD and FND in the b-CQD-FND when the concentration of b-CQD-FND was varied. The fluorescence intensities of both components increased similarly in concentration changes. Fig. 5b shows the relative changes in fluorescence intensity of b-CQD alone and fluorescence ratio of b-CQD-FND. In the concentration range of 20–1000 μg mL−1, the fluorescence intensity of b-CQD changes by 5700 %. In contrast, over the same concentration range, the fluorescence intensity ratio of b-CQD-FND exhibits only a maximum variation of 27 %. Notably, at concentrations above 400 μg mL−1, the intensity ratio remains constant. These results confirm that the effect of concentration changes is remarkably suppressed in b-CQD-FND.

Next, we test the performance of b-CQD-FND as a ratiometric temperature sensor. The fluorescence of CQDs, even within the hybrid structure, demonstrated a significant decrease with increasing temperature. In contrast, FNDs exhibited only a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity under the same conditions, indicating a less pronounced temperature effect (Fig. 5c, left). Consequently, their intensity ratio linearly decreased with increasing temperature, with a temperature sensitivity of −0.57 % °C−1 and a high coefficient of determination (R2 = 0.997), demonstrating its potential as a ratiometric temperature sensor (Fig. 5c, right). This sensitivity is comparable to that of previously reported CQD-based thermometers [27]. It was also confirmed that the fluorescence ratio remained stable even repeated 10 cycles of temperature changes between 25 and 55 °C (Fig. 5d).

In practical biological measurements (such as in cells, tissues, and organisms), it is crucial to accurately measure temperature without being influenced from biological environmental factors such as pH, ion strength, viscosity, or biomolecules. We assessed the impact of these environmental factors on the fluorescence ratio of b-CQD-FND. We defined stable conditions as those in which the fluorescence ratio changed by less than ±3 % compared to that in water. As a result, stable temperature measurements could be achieved with NaCl concentrations up to 1000 mM (Fig. 6 a, left), KCl concentrations up to 1000 mM (Fig. 6a, right), glycerol concentrations up to 10 wt% (Fig. 6b), and within a pH range of 5–11 (Fig. 6c). However, at a BSA concentration of 1000 μg mL−1, the intensity ratio is 13 % higher, indicating an influence on the fluorescence ratio (Fig. 6d, left). Nevertheless, the temperature responsiveness of the probe was maintained even in the presence of this albumin concentration (Fig. 6d, right). This indicates that, with prior calibration, reliable temperature measurements can be achieved in biological environments. Collectively, we demonstrated that b-CQD-FND maintains a temperature sensitivity comparable to conventional CQD-based sensors, while dramatically reducing susceptibility to concentration changes and exhibiting high tolerance to environmental fluctuations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicolor labeling of FNDs and their ODMR measurement
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, we demonstrate that the hybridization of FNDs with CQDs enables multicolor labeling of FNDs for ODMR measurements. By measuring ODMR of FNDs, it is possible to measure physical parameters (e.g. temperature, viscosity, and radical etc.) at the nanoscale. Since all FNDs emit only red fluorescence, distinguishing individual particles poses a significant challenge for multiparameter measurements. To address this issue, CQDs with distinct fluorescence colors (e.g., blue and green emission) were utilized as fluorescent labels to enable clear discrimination of FND particles. In addition to b-CQD-FND, we synthesized green-emitting CQDs (g-CQDs) and conjugated them with FNDs. Using APS, citric acid, and p-phenylenediamine as precursors, we synthesized g-CQDs with a maximum emission wavelength of 500 nm (Fig. S4). The maximum emission wavelengths of b-CQD and g-CQD differ by approximately 100 nm, making it possible to distinguish CQD-labeled FNDs from their fluorescence (Fig. 7 a and b). g-CQD-FND was measured by confocal microscopy and XPS, the conjugation was confirmed (Fig. S5).

Next, based on the fluorescence spectra in Fig. 7a, we simulated the discrimination of multicolor CQD-FNDs (Fig. 7c–f). When both b-CQD-FND and g-CQD-FND are present within the observation field, it is not possible to distinguish them under 532 nm excitation light. However, by imaging the fluorescence regions highlighted in blue in Fig. 7a, b-CQD-FND can be visualized with approximately 10 times higher intensity than the other. Similarly, by imaging the regions highlighted in green, g-CQD-FND can be visualized. Thus, distinguishing the particles in this manner enables discrimination between different FND particles, making it possible to measure different physical parameters from the ODMR measurements of each particle.

We then confirmed that the ODMR signal can be obtained from the CQD-FNDs. There is a concern that the dangling bonds of CQDs could potentially influence the spin state of NVCs, raising the possibility of adverse impacts on the ODMR signal. Since ODMR is essential for measuring physical parameters, it is crucial to ensure that the signal remains observable after conjugation with CQDs. Our findings revealed no such undesirable influence was observed, likely due to the silica coating maintaining an appropriate distance between the FND and CQDs (Fig. 8 a, upper). The ODMR spectrum has been reported to shift towards lower frequencies with increasing temperature [28]. We measured the temperature-induced ODMR frequency shift for b-CQD-FND and g-CQD-FND particles immobilized on a coverslip. As a result, the sensitivity to temperature was determined to be −115 kHz °C−1. We successfully observed frequency shifts for both hybrid particles, confirming their functionality as ODMR sensors (Fig. 8a, lower).

Next, to demonstrate that these hybrids can be applied to biological measurements, we introduced them into cells and C. elegans and verified whether ODMR measurements were feasible. By adding CQD-FND to the cell culture medium and incubating for 24 h, we confirmed that both types of CQD-FND were successfully internalized by the cells. Furthermore, no undesirable changes in cell proliferation or morphology were observed over 5 days after treatment with 50 μg mL−1 of b-CQD-FND and g-CQD-FND – concentrations higher than those used in experiments such as ODMR spectroscopy in HeLa cells – and the cells reached confluence. These results suggest that the potential cytotoxicity of the hybrid is minimal (Fig. S6). We subsequently obtained ODMR signals from the internalized single CQD-FNDs, demonstrating the feasibility of intracellular cell measurements (Fig. 8b). Additionally, by mixing these hybrids with E. coli used as food and feeding them to C. elegans, we obtained ODMR signals from the hybrids within the intestine of the C. elegans (Fig. 8c). Compared to the signals obtained in cells, the ODMR spectrum appeared broadened. This broadening is thought to reflect the rotation of the hybrids caused by ciliary movement in the intestine [29]. These results demonstrate that ODMR measurements are possible with the CQD-labeled FNDs both in cell and in vivo.

We successfully developed a novel hybrid nanosensor combining CQDs and FNDs, which overcomes limitations inherent in CQDs and FNDs as individual temperature sensor. The design of the CQD-FND hybrid, where CQDs and FNDs are conjugated via a silica shell, was critical to preserving both the fluorescence properties of CQDs and the ODMR of FNDs. Fluorescence ratiometric temperature measurements using b-CQD-FND demonstrated a linear response with a sensitivity of −0.57 % °C−1, indicating reliable ratiometric thermometry. Notably, this method exhibited reduced susceptibility to concentration variations and high tolerance to environmental factors such as ionic strength, viscosity, and pH thereby enhancing its applicability in complex biological environments. Additionally, the CQD labeling strategy enabled multicolor identification of individual FNDs, allowing simultaneous quantum sensing via ODMR in both cultured cells and C. elegans.

One of the key applications of this hybrid system is intracellular optical thermometry. In recent years, thermal biology has emphasized the importance of developing innovative temperature probes [30]. Understanding the complex relationship between biological phenomena and temperature requires integrating and interpreting data from multiple temperature probes, making the development of new temperature measurement tools an urgent need. One of the key challenges moving forward is to further enhance temperature sensitivity. Fortunately, recent advances in machine learning have opened new avenues for predicting and designing CQD properties, enabling the rational development of more sensitive quantum dots [31]. We believe that this CQD-FND hybrid system holds significant promise as a novel cellular temperature probe, playing a pivotal role in advancing this field.

Another promising application lies in the field of quantum biosensing. The multicolor labeling of FNDs with b-CQDs and g-CQDs facilitates simultaneous measurements of distinct physical parameters, paving the way for advanced multiparameter ODMR sensing applications. Although FNDs labeled with fluorescent small dye molecules have been reported [32], such dyes suffer from photobleaching, limiting their suitability for long-term measurements and failing to take full advantage of the photostability of FNDs. In comparison, CQD labeling offers significant advantages including the ability to maintain fluorescence properties over extended periods, making it more suitable for long-term and in vivo applications. As discussed in the introduction, b-CQD-FND has the potential to measure temperature in mitochondria, while g-CQD-FND could assess viscosity in liquid droplets, enabling the possibility of evaluating how mitochondrial heat generation affects the viscosity of liquid droplets. We believe these carbon hybrids will contribute to wide range of biological applications.

Kota Shiraya: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yurina Nakane: Data curation. Hiroshi Abe: Resources, Data curation. Takeshi Ohshima: Supervision, Resources. Takuma Sugi: Validation, Supervision, Methodology. Yumi Yoshida: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Kohji Maeda: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Shingo Sotoma: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Shingo Sotoma reports equipment, drugs, or supplies was provided by Kyoto Institute of Technology. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge Dr. Yoshiyuki Nonoguchi for his kind support with the FTIR and Raman spectroscopy measurements. This work was supported by Early-Career Scientists (19K16089 and 21K15053 to S.S.), Iketani Science and Technology Foundation (0351017-A to S.S.), JSPS KAKENHI Grant-in-Aid for Transformative Research Areas B (JP23H03845 to T.S.), for Scientific Research (A) (24H00577 to T.S.), JST-FOREST Program (JPMJFR214R to T.S.).
